question_title: Will the D.C. Circuit stay Judge Cooper's Kennedy Center name-removal injunction before June 14, 2026?
summary: This question decomposes into: P(appeal + stay motion filed) × P(D.C. Circuit grants at least an administrative stay). Strong signals from the Kennedy Center and DOJ indicate appeal is very likely (~88%), and the June 12 compliance deadline creates a forcing function. If a stay motion is filed, the D.C. Circuit has a high admin stay grant rate and recently granted one in the parallel White House ballroom case (~72% conditional probability). However, the statutory case against defendants is unusually strong (clear text: only Congress can rename), and the naming portion was a final judgment on summary judgment — a higher bar than a preliminary injunction. The 5-day delay without filing and Burgum's equivocation introduce some uncertainty about whether the legal route will be pursued. Combined probability: ~63% YES.

factors.description: Kennedy Center and DOJ have explicitly signaled intent to appeal. Kennedy Center VP Roma Daravi confirmed plans; DOJ stated intent to defend on appeal. Trump administration has been extremely aggressive about emergency stays (15-25+ in D.C. Circuit in early 2025).
June 12 compliance deadline creates enormous urgency. If defendants want to prevent name removal, they MUST file before then. This forcing function makes inaction very unlikely.

D.C. Circuit has high administrative stay grant rate. Even a single judge can grant. Admin stay in parallel White House ballroom case shows D.C. Circuit willingness to grant stays in similar Trump-era D.C. property cases.

The statutory argument against defendants is very strong - 94-page opinion, clear statutory text (only Congress can rename). This makes a substantive stay harder to justify on likelihood-of-success prong.

5 days since ruling with no appeal filed yet (though only 2 business days). Burgum equivocated on compliance. Trump suggested Congressional transfer route. Some uncertainty about whether they will pursue legal vs political strategy.
The naming portion was decided on summary judgment (permanent injunction/final order), not preliminary injunction. Higher bar for staying a final judgment than a preliminary one.
D.C. Circuit panel composition includes Trump appointees (Rao, Walker, etc.) who have been sympathetic to administration positions in other cases. (Partially overlaps with admin stay grant rate.)

reasoning:
## Forecast: Will the D.C. Circuit stay the Kennedy Center name-removal injunction before June 14, 2026?

**Probability: 63% YES**

### Key Reasoning

This question requires two things to happen:

1. **The administration must file an appeal + emergency stay motion** (~88% likely)
   - Kennedy Center and DOJ have both signaled intent to appeal
   - The June 12 compliance deadline creates urgency — inaction means Trump's name comes down
   - The Trump DOJ has filed 15-25+ emergency stay motions in the D.C. Circuit in 2025 alone
   - Main risk: pivot to political strategy (Congressional transfer) or internal disagreement

2. **The D.C. Circuit must grant at least an administrative stay** (~72% likely if filed)
   - Administrative stays are nearly routine (~80% grant rate)
   - The D.C. Circuit granted one in the parallel White House ballroom case
   - Can be issued by a single judge within hours of filing
   - Counterweight: The statutory text is unusually clear-cut (only Congress can rename), and this was a final judgment, not a preliminary injunction

**Combined: 88% × 72% ≈ 63%**

### Sensitivity Range
- **Low scenario** (less aggressive filing, harder stay): ~45%
- **Base scenario**: ~63%
- **High scenario** (very aggressive filing, routine admin stay): ~78%
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the D.C. Circuit stay Judge Cooper's Kennedy Center name-removal injunction before June 14, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43759)
[Trump's name likely to remain on Kennedy Center pending expected appeal](https://spectrumnews1.com/oh/columbus/news/2026/06/01/kennedy-center-beatty-appeal)
[Trump slams federal judges over ballroom delay, Kennedy Center setback](https://www.newsweek.com/trump-blasts-judges-after-ballroom-kennedy-center-setbacks-12014446)
[D.C. Circuit's Trump appointees issue another administrative stay to block justice](https://www.lawdork.com/p/dc-circuits-trump-appointees-issue)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, at any time between May 31, 2026 and June 14, 2026 (i.e., before June 14, 2026 UTC), the U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit issues any order — including an administrative stay, a stay pending appeal, or an injunction pending appeal — that stays, suspends, or otherwise pauses the enforcement of the name-removal portion of Judge Christopher R. Cooper's May 29, 2026 preliminary injunction in the Kennedy Center case (Beatty v. Kennedy Center Board of Trustees or its consolidated/related caption). The order must come from the D.C. Circuit (not from the district court itself and not from the Supreme Court). Resolution will be based on the public docket for the appeal on CourtListener (https://www.courtlistener.com/) or PACER, or reporting from [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions). The question resolves NO if no such D.C. Circuit stay order has been entered before June 14, 2026, including cases where (a) no appeal is filed, (b) an appeal is filed but no stay is sought, (c) a stay is sought but denied, or (d) only the district court issues a stay.
fine_print: An order from a single D.C. Circuit judge or motions panel (e.g., an administrative stay) counts. A stay limited to only the closure/renovation portion of the injunction without staying the name-removal portion does NOT count. A stay issued by the Supreme Court on certiorari or via the Circuit Justice does not count for YES (the order must originate from the D.C. Circuit). If the appellants voluntarily comply or the case is mooted (e.g., Congress acts) without a D.C. Circuit stay being entered, the question resolves NO.

factors.description: Kennedy Center and DOJ have explicitly signaled intent to appeal. Kennedy Center VP Roma Daravi confirmed plans; DOJ stated intent to defend on appeal. Trump administration has been extremely aggressive about emergency stays (15-25+ in D.C. Circuit in early 2025).
June 12 compliance deadline creates enormous urgency. If defendants want to prevent name removal, they MUST file before then. This forcing function makes inaction very unlikely.

D.C. Circuit has high administrative stay grant rate (~80%). Even a single judge can grant. Admin stay in parallel White House ballroom case shows D.C. Circuit willingness to grant stays in similar Trump-era D.C. property cases.

The statutory argument against defendants is very strong - 94-page opinion, clear statutory text (only Congress can rename). This makes a substantive stay harder to justify on likelihood-of-success prong.

5 days since ruling with no appeal filed (though only 2 business days). Burgum equivocated. Trump suggested Congressional transfer route. Some uncertainty about whether they'll pursue legal vs political strategy.
The naming portion was decided on summary judgment (permanent injunction/final order), not preliminary injunction. Higher bar for staying a final judgment than a preliminary one.
D.C. Circuit has multiple Trump appointees (Rao, Walker, etc.) who have been sympathetic to administration positions. Panel composition could favor a stay.

input.anchor: Base rate for Trump administration getting an emergency stay from D.C. Circuit when sought: ~50-60% (combining ~80% admin stay grant rate with uncertainty about whether motion will be filed). Administrative stays specifically: ~80% when sought.
input.assessment:
The question decomposes into two sub-questions: (1) Will the administration file an appeal + emergency stay motion targeting the name-removal portion? and (2) Will the D.C. Circuit grant at least an administrative stay?

For (1): Very strong signals suggest filing is likely (~88%). The Kennedy Center and DOJ have both signaled appeal, Trump is publicly combative, and the June 12 deadline creates a forcing function. The main risk is that the administration pivots to a political strategy (Congressional transfer) or internal disagreement causes delay. Only 2 business days have passed since the ruling, so the lack of filing isn't alarming.

For (2): If filed, P(at least administrative stay) ≈ 72%. Administrative stays are nearly routine in the D.C. Circuit (~80% grant rate), and the court granted one in the parallel White House ballroom case. However, the naming ruling was a final judgment on summary judgment, not a preliminary injunction - a higher bar. The statutory text is clear-cut, making it harder to argue likelihood of success on the merits. But administrative stays don't require a full merits analysis.

Combined: P(YES) ≈ 0.88 × 0.72 ≈ 63%. The sensitivity range is 45-78% depending on assumptions about filing probability and grant rate.

Key tension: The Trump DOJ's aggressive track record on stays vs. the unusually strong statutory basis of the ruling.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor is conditional base rate of ~50-60% for D.C. Circuit stays in Trump admin cases. The specific factors here (strong statutory ruling, but also high political salience and deadline urgency) push in both directions. Status quo is NO (no stay = name comes down). I'm at 63%, which is modestly above the base but reflects the strong signals of imminent appeal and the high admin stay grant rate.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Will the administration actually file, or pivot to political/defiance strategy? 2. Will the D.C. Circuit treat this case differently from other Trump admin stays given the clear statutory basis? 3. Is there internal DOJ disagreement about the strength of the case on appeal? 4. What will the June 5 joint status report reveal?
input.tool_audit: Research tool: Very useful, provided comprehensive case details, procedural posture, and historical D.C. Circuit stay rates. Web search: Helpful for finding latest articles but didn't find evidence of any filing between June 1-3. Fetch URL: Some URLs timed out (WashPost) but Spectrum News and Lawdork provided useful information. CourtListener docket: Confirmed no D.C. Circuit filing as of research agent's check. Code execution: Useful for probability decomposition and sensitivity analysis.
input.update_triggers: - News of a formal notice of appeal filing would shift probability up 10-15pp. - News of an emergency stay motion would shift probability up 15-20pp. - If June 5 status report shows no intent to appeal, probability drops 20-25pp. - If Trump explicitly says he'll defy the order rather than appeal, probability drops. - If Congress introduces fast-track legislation to rename the Center, probability drops.
input.process_reflection: The research tool was excellent at gathering the factual background quickly. The decomposition into P(filed) × P(granted|filed) felt natural for this legal question. One challenge: it's hard to find real-time docket updates - the research agent's data was from June 1, and there could have been filings on June 2-3 that I can't see. A specialized legal docket monitoring tool would be helpful. The web search didn't surface any June 2-3 developments, which is informative (absence of evidence that a filing has been reported). The D.C. Circuit stay rate data (~80% admin, ~40-60% substantive) from the research agent was very useful but came from estimated/approximated figures rather than hard data.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Checks Performed

### Resolution Criteria ✅
The agent fetched the full Metaculus question with resolution criteria and engaged with them in detail. The criteria require a D.C. Circuit order (not district court or Supreme Court) staying the name-removal portion specifically. The agent correctly structured its analysis around this.

### Hallucinated Evidence — Minor Issue (Factor 3)
**Factor 3** claims "D.C. Circuit has high administrative stay grant rate (~80%)." The trace shows this figure comes from the research agent's output (sourced to scotusblog.com), and the Lawdork article the agent fetched confirms the D.C. Circuit "loves administrative stays" but does not give a specific percentage. The ~80% number appears to be an estimate embedded in the research agent's synthesis rather than a directly verified statistic. However, the directional claim (high grant rate) is well-supported by the Lawdork article's description and the CECOT case example where a stay was granted within 5 hours. This is a soft number being treated as harder than it is, but the underlying conclusion is grounded. Not a hallucination per se, but the confidence (0.75) on a loosely-sourced statistic is slightly aggressive.

### Double-Counting — Mild Concern (Factors 3 and 7)
**Factor 3** (D.C. Circuit ~80% admin stay grant rate) and **Factor 7** (D.C. Circuit has Trump appointees sympathetic to administration) draw on overlapping evidence. The 80% grant rate was estimated from recent D.C. Circuit behavior, which already reflects the current bench composition including Trump appointees Rao, Walker, etc. Factor 7's logit of +0.5 is adding signal that's already baked into Factor 3's rate. Combined effective logits: Factor 3 contributes +0.75 and Factor 7 contributes +0.30. This matters modestly but doesn't change the direction since the agent's final estimate was substantially below the factor-sum implication anyway (63% vs factor-implied ~89%).

### Cross-Question Consistency — Minor Flag
The agent previously forecasted question 43740 ("Will **any court** stay or reverse **part** of Judge Cooper's Kennedy Center ruling before June 14, 2026?") at 65%. The current question 43759 is strictly narrower: it requires the D.C. Circuit specifically (excluding district court and Supreme Court) and specifically the name-removal portion (excluding closure-only stays). Logically, P(43759) should be ≤ P(43740). The agent has 63% vs 65%, which is directionally correct but the gap seems small given two layers of narrowing. That said, the practical difference is probably modest since D.C. Circuit is the most likely court anyway, and the name-removal portion is the most politically significant issue likely to be stayed. The 2pp gap is defensible.

### Resolution Misalignment — No Issue
The resolution criteria correctly identify that this could be an administrative stay, stay pending appeal, or injunction pending appeal from the D.C. Circuit. The agent's analysis properly considers all three paths, especially emphasizing administrative stays as the most likely route. ✅

### Wrong-Direction Factors — None Found
All factor signs are consistent with their descriptions. Positive logits support YES (appeal filing signals, deadline urgency, high stay rates, sympathetic judges). Negative logits support NO (strong statutory case, delay, summary judgment bar). ✅

### Contradictory Assessment — None Found
The narrative assessment reaches 63% YES, which correctly reflects the multiplicative chain (P(filed) × P(granted|filed)) rather than the additive factor sum. The agent explicitly acknowledges and explains this gap. ✅

### Weak Counterargument — No Issue
The agent's counterargument is substantive: it constructs a coherent alternative narrative for ~40-45% based on (1) the statutory case being exceptionally strong against appellants, (2) Burgum's equivocation being more informative than the spokesperson's statement, (3) administrative stays possibly not being automatic for clear-cut statutory cases, and (4) filing delay being informative. This is a genuine adversarial argument. ✅

### Probability Assessment — Reasonable
My independent estimate: P(filed) ~85-90% × P(admin stay granted | filed) ~65-75% = ~55-68%, centering around 60%. The agent's 63% falls comfortably within this range. No meaningful divergence.

### Tool Audit Check
The agent's tool audit mentions "Some URLs timed out (WashPost)" — the metrics show 4 fetch_url calls with 1 error (25%), which matches the WashPost timeout visible in the trace. The audit also says "no tool failures" is not claimed. Consistent. ✅

### Overall Verdict: WARN
The double-counting between Factors 3 and 7 is a real issue, and the ~80% administrative stay grant rate is a soft number treated with moderate-high confidence. Neither error changes the forecast direction or substantially affects the final probability (since the agent already discounted from the factor sum to 63% via the multiplicative decomposition). The overall analysis is thorough and well-reasoned.
